title: Crème Brûlée
subtitle: Vanilla cream custard baked in a bain-marie with a caramelised sugar top
excerpt: Rich cream custard set gently in a bain-marie until just wobbling, then chilled and finished with a layer of caster sugar torched to a thin, crackling caramel. The contrast between the brittle top and the cold, trembling custard beneath is what makes this dessert.
sourceNotes: Crème brûlée has a long and disputed history across French, British, and Spanish cooking. The French name and modern form are associated with French haute cuisine of the seventeenth century onward. The defining feature, a caramelised sugar crust on a set cream custard, appears under various names in British, French, and Catalan cookery.

content.text: The custard needs to set slowly and gently. High heat gives a grainy, scrambled texture; the water bath in the oven keeps the temperature of the custard below boiling point throughout baking. The aim is a custard that is just set around the edges and still trembles faintly at the very centre when shaken, it will firm up further as it chills.

The sugar layer for the brûlée must be thin, a scant teaspoon per ramekin spread as evenly as possible. Too thick and the caramel will be glass-like and difficult to crack; too thin and it will burn before it sets. Torch in smooth, continuous circles, keeping the flame moving until the sugar is uniformly amber. Serve immediately.

items.name: Caster sugar
items.prepNote: for the brûlée top, about 1 tsp per ramekin

content.text: Six 150 ml shallow ramekins. Shallow ramekins give more surface area for the caramel and a better custard-to-sugar ratio than deep ones.

Heat the oven to 150°C fan. Put {{double-cream}} of double cream and the vanilla pod and seeds into a medium saucepan. Heat over low heat until just below simmering, small bubbles at the edges, no rolling boil. Remove from the heat and leave to infuse for 10 minutes. Whisk 6 egg yolks with 100 g of caster sugar in a large bowl until pale and slightly thickened. Pour the warm cream over the egg mixture in a slow stream, whisking constantly. Strain through a fine sieve into a jug.

Place 6 ramekins in a deep roasting tin. Pour the custard evenly into the ramekins. Pour enough just-boiled water into the tin to come halfway up the sides of the ramekins. Transfer carefully to the oven and bake for 35, 40 minutes until the custards are just set at the edges with a faint wobble at the very centre. Remove from the water bath and cool to room temperature. Refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or overnight.

Blot any condensation from the surface of each custard with kitchen paper. Scatter about 1 heaped teaspoon of caster sugar (from the 40 g reserved) over each ramekin and tilt to spread it evenly. Using a kitchen torch, caramelise the sugar with continuous circular movements, keeping the flame 3, 4 cm from the surface, until the sugar is uniformly amber and glossy. Serve immediately, the caramel will soften within 15 minutes.

items.fix: Let the cream cool for 10 minutes after heating before mixing with the yolks. Strain the mixture before pouring into the ramekins. Keep the oven at 150°C fan, higher temperatures cause curdling.
items.cause: Cream was too hot when mixed with the eggs, or the oven temperature was too high
items.symptom: Custard has a grainy or scrambled texture

items.fix: Use just-boiled water, not cold water. The water level should come halfway up the ramekins. Continue baking in 5-minute increments, a small wobble at the very centre is correct; liquid is not.
items.cause: Water in the bain-marie was not hot enough, or too much water was used
items.symptom: Custard still liquid in the centre after 40 minutes

items.fix: Use only 1 scant teaspoon of sugar per ramekin and spread it as thinly as possible. Keep the torch moving constantly. If part of the surface is burning, pull the torch back and continue circling.
items.cause: Layer is too thick, or the torch is held too close
items.symptom: Sugar for the brûlée is burning rather than caramelising evenly

items.fix: Serve within 10 minutes of torching. Use a full teaspoon of sugar per ramekin, a paper-thin layer won't set hard enough to crack.
items.cause: Served too long after torching, or the sugar layer was too thin
items.symptom: Caramel layer is soft and not cracking

content.text: The origin of crème brûlée is disputed. French, British, and Catalan food historians all have a claim. A similar dish called burnt cream appears in English manuscripts from the seventeenth century. Crema catalana, with its caramelised sugar crust, has been recorded in Catalonia since the eighteenth century. The French version became a fixture of restaurant menus in the twentieth century. It is now one of the most recognised French desserts outside France.
